name: Microeconomic Theory

name: How Ideas Are Made
description: What are ideas, and what are they for? In daily life, we encounter a wide variety of ideas expressed in diverse media, from sentences and paragraphs to maps and diagrams. name: The Idea of the Self: Philosophical Perspectives

name: Philosophy of Love and Friendship

name: Mind and Body

name: Philosophy of Race and Racism

name: Philosophy and Medicine

name: Self & Community
description: This course invites students to discern their insights--that is, the ways that they come to knowledge--for the sake of building a good life and a good world. It is based on Bernard Lonergan's "heuristic method," a method of discovery of the inner structures of our understanding that shape ou

name: Senior Honors Seminar
description: Restricted to departmental honors students only. The senior honors seminar will support the development of a senior thesis. Topics will include methods for strong research, writing workshops, and contemporary philosophical readings and discussion.

name: Perspectives Seminar
description: By Arrangement

name: Lonergan's Critical Realism: Insight and Beyond
description: Bernard Lonergan, S.J. (1904-1984) was a philosophical and religious thinker deeply concerned about the philosophical, ethical, socio-political, and religious situation of the twentieth century. That situation was defined by confusion over whether knowledge was possible, what it consisted in, and ho

name: Medieval Philosophy
description: Far from being monolithic and repetitive, the Middle Ages were a creative period during which multiple solutions were tried to make sense of the world and of human life. The legacy of Antiquity, the philosophic and scientific knowledge of the time, and religious views were combined in original synth

name: Mahayana Buddhism: Thought and Practice
description: This course explores Mahayana Buddhist thought, meditation practice, narrative, and ritual practice across Asia. We consider texts ancient and modern. After a basic introduction to Buddhism, we take up topics ranging from meditation, to compassion, nirvana, emptiness, Buddha nature, Zen, and Mahayan

name: Kant's Religion Within the Boundary of Mere Reason
description: This course aims to read Immanuel Kant's work Religion within the boundary of mere reason in its entirety, along with some other texts related to this work. In these writings, Kant addresses several topics of great importance to his practical philosophy: the idea of radical evil, the significance of

name: Do Robots Have Souls?
description: Do robots have souls? Probably not. But this course thinks its a good question, and therefore explores our fundamental thinking of technology and the ways in which it overlaps with religious and philosophical thinking of animal, inanimate object, technical object, and human. The philosophic core is

name: Figures in Analytic Philosophy
description: This course examines the evolution of Anglo-American philosophy throughout the 20th century, with particular emphasis on the development of analytical philosophy. Central to our inquiry will be an epistemological and metaphysical analysis of pivotal themes through the works of major figures such as

name: Modern Philosophy
description: From the early 17th to the end of the 18th century, reason asserted its autonomy and triumphed in the Age of Enlightenment, but at the same time reflected on its own limits. Through a series of themes and problems, the course presents the major philosophies (roughly, from Descartes to Kant) that hav

name: Crisis and Renewal in Early 20th-Century Europe
description: The term crisis appears often in the philosophical discourse of the past century. In this class, we will explore its meaning with European thinkers who have been influential in that conversation. In so doing, we will question various aspects of a crisis in culture, including the scientific, politica

name: Philosophically Religious 1
description: The 19 th century witnesses major changes in the concept of religion with the rise of atheism, modern philosophy, and science. This course covers philosophical approaches to religion from theologians and philosophers both: what is faith for John Henry Newman, Sren Kierkegaard, and Friedrich Schleier

name: Capstone: Working, Playing, Being
description: Capstone classes may NOT be taken Pass/Fail. You may take only ONE Capstone class before graduation. Seniors only.. The conventional wisdom is that BC student culture is "work hard, play hard." But what do we mean by "work" and "play" here? How do these implicit meanings reflect broader cultural for

name: The Ethics of Peace and War
description: This course will explore historical, ethical, and political issues concerned with peace and war. We will examine the just war tradition, beginning in the medieval period, as well as recent developments in the field, such as topics in transitional justice. While the main emphasis will be on philosoph

name: Comparative Mysticism
description: The area of mysticism or spirituality has been the focus of a heated debate among those who argue for the universality and the particularity of mystical experiences. In this course, we shall engage in this discussion by studying the writings of important mystics from various religious traditions.

name: Philosophical Implications of Artificial Intelligence
description: This course will examine the philosophical presuppositions of artificial intelligence and new technologies. The philosophical framework in which we will move is the Aristotelian-Thomistic conceptual context in order to further understand and make explicit the ramifications of topics such as machine

name: Faith and Reason
description: The aim of this course is to critically evaluate the relationship between faith and reason, as a fundamental distinction which runs through the history of Western philosophical thought. From a classical perspective, faith is a type of knowledge which is not based on empirical evidence, a choice whic

name: Philosophy of Existence
description: An introduction to the main questions of existentialist philosophy from Kierkegaard and Nietzsche to Heidegger, Sartre and Camus. The major issues dealt with include freedom and determinism, desire and death, anxiety and the search for the absolute.

name: Philosophy of Imagination
description: Readings in the philosophy of imagination from ancient myth to post-modernity. Beginning with Biblical and Greek accounts of images and image-making, this course will explore three main paradigm shifts in the western history of imagination: (1) the ancient paradigm of the Mirror (Plato to Augustine)

name: Philosophy of Language
description: This course will consider major texts and movements in 20th century philosophy of language in both the analytic and continental traditions, reading the work of Russell, Wittgenstein, J. L. Austin, Quine and Davidson as well as Ricoeur and Derrida . Our goal will be to bring together these very diffe

name: Skepticism, Stoicism, and Neo-Platonism
description: Ancient philosophy is the period following Aristotle and stretching into the third century A.D.,Hellenistic and Roman Philosophy. name: Metaphysics
description: The course begins with classical modern philosophers. Their problems concern the relation of mind and body, the possibility of objective knowledge, and cause and effect. Their method is that of science, combining both empirical and logical elements. After these modern thinkers, giving our cultural a

name: Selected Readings in Phenomenology

description: This course examines normal modal logics (logics of necessity and possibility) and the logic of inferences involving probabilities. name: African American Philosophy
description: This course will involve an examination of African-American philosophy as well as contemporary philosophical approaches to race, gender, anti-racism, democracy, and the resistance to oppression with specific regard to Black experiences in the Americas. name: Law and Interpretation
description: An exploration of philosophical theories of interpretation (e.g.,Barthes, Gadamer, etc.), legal theories of interpretation, especially with respect to constitutionalism (e.g., Scalia, Dworkin, etc.), and selected American court cases.

name: The Philosophy of Alain LeRoy Locke
description: The course will explore Alain Locke's philosophical work. name: Phenomenology of Perception
description: The course is designed as a monographic introduction to Merleau-Ponty's Phenomenology of Perception. We will begin by situating Merleau-Ponty's project in relation to Husserl's phenomenology and will read Merleau-Ponty's own book for the remainder of the semester. name: Frederick Douglass in Philosophical Dialogue

name: Plato's Theaetetus
description: The purpose of this course is to explore Platos consideration of perception and memory in the Theaetetus. name: Paul Ricoeur: A Philosopher reads the Bible
description: The French philosopher Paul Ricoeur is considered, along with Hans Georg Gadamer, the founder of contemporary hermeneutic philosophy. name: Heidegger and Politics

name: Rationality and Religious Commitment

name: Hermeneutics of Christian Life
description: The course will proceed in four parts. 1. Methodological clarification and concentration on the question of a phenomenality proper to Christian life; 2.
